title: Mulligatawny soup
subtitle: Anglo-Indian spiced lentil and chicken soup
excerpt: A thick, spiced soup of red lentils, chicken, and apple with coconut milk and a restrained amount of heat — invented by British cooks in colonial India and refined by several generations since. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Mulligatawny is an Anglo-Indian creation derived from the Tamil dish rasam (a thin pepper water). The name comes from the Tamil milagu thanni (pepper water). The thick, meat-and-lentil version developed in the colonial period through British adaptations. Documented by Lizzie Collingham in Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors (2005) and Madhur Jaffrey in A Taste of India (1985).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the coconut milk for up to three months. Stir in coconut milk on reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Keeps refrigerated for four days.

content.text: Mulligatawny sits somewhere between a soup and a light curry. The lentils dissolve into the base and thicken it; the apple gives a subtle tartness that lifts the spicing; the coconut milk rounds everything off. It should have definite heat from the spices but not aggression. The shredded chicken is optional — this version can be made without it as a vegetable soup.

items.prepNote: poached and shredded, or use leftover cooked chicken

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, {{turmeric}} of ground turmeric, and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes; stir for 1 minute. Add {{apple-eating}} diced apple and stir for 2 minutes. Add {{red-lentils}} of rinsed red lentils and pour in {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to the boil, reduce heat, and simmer for 20 minutes until the lentils are completely soft. Use a stick blender to blend about half the soup to thicken it while keeping some texture. Stir in {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and {{garam-masala}} of garam masala. Add the shredded chicken if using and warm through for 3 minutes. Squeeze in the lemon juice, taste for seasoning, and serve with coriander.

items.symptom: Soup too thick, almost like porridge
items.cause: Too many lentils for the quantity of stock, or blended too thoroughly.
items.fix: Add more stock and stir well. Only blend about half the soup — the texture should be thick but still have some body.

items.cause: Apple variety too tart, or added too much.
items.fix: One eating apple per four portions is correct. Bramley (cooking apple) is too sharp here; use a Cox or similar eating apple.

items.cause: Not cooked long enough in the oil before the liquid went in.
items.fix: The spice paste needs at least 1 minute of frying in the oil — it should smell fragrant before the lentils and stock are added.

content.text: Mulligatawny is colonial cuisine at its most transparent: an attempt by British cooks in India to recreate the familiar format of a meat soup using the spices and ingredients around them. The result is a dish that belongs fully to neither tradition but has its own distinct character. It returned to Britain with the colonial population and became a fixture of Victorian and Edwardian upper-class households, appearing in Mrs Beeton and in menus from the grand hotels of the era. It has survived that association and settled into British cooking as a warming, slightly exotic winter soup.
